summary:
The manuscript demonstrates a distinctive narrative voice, compelling central characters, and a thematic engine that works — but it cannot reach shortlist level at its current prose-formula density. Three syntactic templates dominate the sentence architecture across all eighteen chapters: negation-correction pairs ('not X but Y' / 'not X — Y', ~197 instances), appositive-explanation clauses ('which was [gloss]', ~160 instances), and comparative-deflection constructions ('the way [noun] [verb]', ~124 instances). At a combined density of roughly one formulaic construction every nine lines, the machinery of the prose becomes audible by Chapter 3 and dominant by Chapter 9, undermining the naturalistic voice the novel otherwise achieves. A cold reader will begin hearing the narrator's explanatory reflex — the compulsion to gloss every observation with a qualification, comparison, or negation-correction — as a tic rather than a style, and by mid-book this reflex flattens the rhythmic variety the style bible demands.

A secondary structural problem: Chapter 14's three-city tour (Cleveland, Pittsburgh, Detroit) restages the same performance-failure insight — scripted material dies, spontaneity works briefly, the return to script kills it — three times in a single chapter without sufficient new dramatic pressure in the later cities to justify the repetition. Chapter 12's profile chapter similarly sprawls, restating the 'Doug is ordinary and the world keeps narrating him as extraordinary' beat across multiple scenes that do not add distinct dramatic turns.

Additionally, ten chapters contain verbatim or near-verbatim style-bible calibration lines explicitly marked 'DO NOT COPY, PARAPHRASE, OR ECHO IN ANY DRAFT' — including 'I drive a Civic,' 'Tell me something funny,' 'I bought better cereal,' 'I'm funnier on paper. You're funnier in person,' and others. Chapter 13 places itself in 'late April' despite following the May-dated Chapter 12, violating the continuity sheet's sequential timeline.

The novel's strengths are real: the shampoo-on-the-ledge scene (Ch11), the spreadsheet revelation (Ch13), the Gloria chapter (Ch10), and the restrained ending (Ch18) are all at or near shortlist quality. Doug's voice — the man who processes the world through insurance language because he cannot process it through feeling-language — is genuinely original. The comedy is character-driven, the ending refuses moral summary, and the thematic engine (spontaneity vs. performance, documentation vs. feeling) is sound. Fixing the identified prose patterns, replacing the leaked calibration lines, correcting the timeline, and tightening the tour and profile chapters would bring this manuscript to shortlist consideration.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2008, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2028, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2030, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2032, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2060, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2078, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2106, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2142
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. This chapter (the Indianapolis keynote and Holiday Inn aftermath) is one of the most narration-heavy in the novel and concentrates all three dominant prose patterns. Negation-correction pairs: 'not a wave but a series of individual decisions' (2008), 'Not comfort. Not discomfort. The mattress equivalent of fine' (2032). Appositive-explanation: 'adequate, in the way Roy used that word' (2028), 'the particular kind of quiet that Roy's house was' (2060). The chapter uses 'the way' comparisons at least 8 times and 'which was' appositives at least 10 times. The hotel room scene (2026-2156) is especially saturated — virtually every observation is followed by an explanatory or qualifying clause. The patterns appear across all 18 chapters at a combined density of ~one every 9 lines; this chapter is among the worst 7. The KEEP instance for this chapter is line 2120 ('The structure is sound and the answer is still no') — a strong insurance metaphor that earns its comparison. All other formulaic constructions in this chapter should be REPLACED with varied syntax.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2000-2156. In the hotel room scene, cut at least 60% of negation-correction pairs by letting the first term stand alone without the corrective second term — e.g., at line 2032, keep 'The mattress equivalent of fine' but drop the preceding 'Not comfort. Not discomfort.' frame. Replace at least half of 'the way [noun] [verb]' comparisons with direct description — e.g., at line 2028, instead of 'adequate, in the way Roy used that word,' try 'Adequate. Roy's word.' or simply remove the comparison. For 'which was' appositives, delete the explanatory clause when the observation is already clear — e.g., at line 2030, the bedspread pattern description is strong enough without the 'the color of a decision made in a conference room' extension. Keep the insurance metaphor at line 2120 ('filing a claim you know is going to be denied') — it is the chapter's strongest figure and earns its length. Aim for no more than 3 negation-correction pairs and 4 'which was' appositives in the entire chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 2000-2156. Count negation-correction pairs ('not X but Y' or 'Not X. Y.'): pass if 5 or fewer in the chapter (down from ~15). Count 'which was' appositives: pass if 6 or fewer (down from ~10). Count 'the way [noun] [verb]' comparisons: pass if 4 or fewer (down from ~8). The insurance metaphor at line 2120 must be preserved. At least three observations in the hotel room scene should land without any qualifying clause — the detail should sit alone and trust the reader.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2500, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2536, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2611, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2674, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2719, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2741, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2761, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2805
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 11 (the deep Simone sessions, the shampoo revelation) is a strong chapter whose best moments are undercut by the narrator's reflexive glossing. The emotional-weight seating formula is especially concentrated here: 'the room would shift slightly' (2611), 'the word sat between them. Managing.' (2741), 'everything in the notebook was material, and material was what went on stage' (2761). The negation-correction pattern appears in Doug's interiority repeatedly: 'felt like more than the situation called for' (2500), 'not absence but attention' patterns. The 'which was' appositive appears ~12 times. The chapter's best passage — the shampoo story (2743-2759) — is largely free of these patterns, which proves the voice works without them. KEEP the shampoo section's spare syntax as the chapter's model. REPLACE the formulaic constructions elsewhere.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2494-2810. Use the shampoo passage (2743-2759) as the rhythmic template for the rest of the chapter — direct sentences, specific nouns, no qualifying clauses. In the cereal section (2555-2610), cut at least half the 'which was' and 'the way' constructions — Doug's precision should come through word choice, not through appositives explaining his word choice. In the section after the shampoo story (2757-2810), reduce the narrator's processing of what just happened — the pen cap on the counter is a perfect final image and doesn't need the preceding paragraph's explicit statement that 'there was no wall between the material and the man' (2761). That line is thesis language. Let the pen cap carry it. Aim for no more than 4 negation-correction pairs, 5 'which was' appositives, and 3 emotional-weight seating constructions in the full chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 2494-2810. The shampoo passage (2743-2759) must be preserved largely intact. The thesis statement at line 2761 ('the understanding that there was no wall between the material and the man') must be cut or replaced with concrete scene action. Count formulaic constructions: pass if negation-correction pairs are 6 or fewer, 'which was' appositives are 7 or fewer, and no paragraph contains more than 2 of these constructions in sequence. The chapter's final image (the pen cap) must land without preceding explanatory narration.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2886, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2900, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2990, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3000, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3008, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3066, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3074, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3078
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 12 (the Alex Chen profile) is the longest chapter in the novel and is heavily narrated throughout. The 'the way' comparison appears at high density: 'looked at the interior the way Doug had seen adjusters look at damaged property' (2886), 'found significance the way Doug's apartment found pollen' (3000). Negation-correction pairs accumulate in Doug's reaction to the profile: 'not a philosophy. It was structural.' (3042), 'not artlessly, but without reaching for beauty' (implied patterns throughout). The post-profile reflection section (3062-3124) is the worst — nearly every sentence in Doug's reaction to the article contains a corrective or qualifying clause. The 'which was' appositive appears ~14 times in the chapter. KEEP the pollen comparison at line 3000 — it is the chapter's strongest figure. REPLACE elsewhere.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2812-3126. The profile-reaction section (3062-3124) is the primary target. Doug's paragraph-by-paragraph correction of Alex's article (3066-3078) uses the same syntactic move repeatedly: 'The article said X. This was Y.' Vary the syntax — some corrections should be single sentences, some should be elliptical, some should just be Doug's thought without the article's frame. In the shadowing sections (2884-3060), cut at least half of the 'the way [noun] [verb]' constructions — Alex observing Doug doesn't need to be constantly compared to other modes of observation. The pollen comparison (3000) may stay. Aim for no more than 4 negation-correction pairs, 6 'which was' appositives, and 5 'the way' comparisons in the full chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 2812-3126. Count 'the way [noun] [verb]' comparisons: pass if 7 or fewer (down from ~12). The profile-reaction section (3062-3124) must contain at least 3 observations that stand without any corrective frame — Doug reacts without needing to explain how the article got him wrong. The pollen comparison (3000) must be preserved.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3207, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3251, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3261, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3283, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3341, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3409, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3411
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 13 (the spreadsheet revelation) is one of the novel's strongest chapters, but the emotional-weight seating formula is especially concentrated here: 'The word landed on the table between them — divorce' (3207), 'They just sat there side by side like competing coverage interpretations' (3261), 'the sentence sat in the air between them' (3283). The 'sat between them' construction appears 4 times in this single chapter — it is the novel's default way of marking a significant moment, and by its fourth use in the same scene, the reader anticipates the move. The closing reflection (3405-3419) also restates the spreadsheet's significance through thesis language: 'He had turned his daughter into a line item' (3411) — which is powerful but arrives after the scene has already shown this through Kelsey's words ('I was a case file,' 3253). KEEP the emotional-weight construction at line 3207 ('divorce' landing on the table) — it is the first and strongest use in the chapter. REPLACE the other three instances and cut the thesis restatement at 3411.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3127-3420. Keep the 'landed on the table' construction at line 3207 — it works because it's the chapter's first emotional detonation. At line 3261, replace 'sat there side by side like competing coverage interpretations' with a physical detail — what Doug's body is doing, what the restaurant sounds like. At line 3283, replace 'the sentence sat in the air between them' with silence or specific sensory detail. At line 3411 ('He had turned his daughter into a line item'), cut this sentence — Kelsey's line at 3253 ('I was a case file') already delivered this insight with more force. The parking-lot reflection (3405-3419) should be trimmed by roughly a third; the ceramic cat and the faded awning are good images but the passage restates the chapter's emotional thesis when the lunch scene has already landed it. Aim for only 1 emotional-weight seating construction in the chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 3127-3420. Count emotional-weight seating constructions ('sat between them,' 'sat in the air,' 'landed on the table'): pass if 2 or fewer (down from 4). The sentence 'He had turned his daughter into a line item' at line 3411 must be cut. The parking-lot reflection (3405-3419) must be 20+ lines shorter than current. The 'divorce' landing at line 3207 must be preserved. Kelsey's line 'I was a case file' (3253) must remain the chapter's sharpest articulation of the spreadsheet's meaning.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3450, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3504, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3524, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3528, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3624, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3630, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3636, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3683
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 14 (the three-city tour) is the second-longest chapter and accumulates formulaic constructions across three performance venues and three post-show reflections. The negation-correction pattern appears in nearly every performance description: 'Not absent — wrong' (3450), 'not laughter exactly, but attention' (3528/3624), 'not a gap so much as a different country' (3582). The post-show driving scenes repeat the 'he thought about X. He thought about Y.' inventory pattern (3636-3683) — listing what Doug is processing instead of showing it through detail. The 'which was' appositive appears ~11 times. The 'the way' comparison appears ~8 times. KEEP the negation-correction at line 3624 ('not laughter, but the attention you give to something true') — it captures the chapter's strongest moment. REPLACE elsewhere.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3422-3688. The driving sections between cities (3498-3510, 3553-3560) and the final drive home (3634-3688) are the primary targets for formula reduction. The 'he thought about' inventory at lines 3636-3683 should be cut by at least half — instead of listing every topic Doug processes, pick 2-3 and render them with specificity. At line 3683, the depreciation metaphor ('the damage was structural, not cosmetic') is earned but the sentence that follows ('no amount of rehearsal was going to bring the depreciated value of his performance up to replacement cost') extends the metaphor past its landing — cut the extension. Aim for no more than 5 negation-correction pairs in the full chapter (down from ~12) and no more than 5 'which was' appositives (down from ~11).
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 3422-3688. Count negation-correction pairs: pass if 7 or fewer. The driving reflection at lines 3636-3683 must be at least 15 lines shorter than current. The depreciation metaphor at line 3683 must end at 'structural, not cosmetic' — the extension must be cut. The 'not laughter, but attention' formulation at line 3624 must be preserved.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3695, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3751, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3797, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3819, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3855, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3901, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3913
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 15 (the bar with Simone) concentrates the 'the way' comparison and negation-correction patterns in Doug's observation of Simone and his interiority. The insurance-metaphor extended comparisons are especially heavy here: 'claims from the same weather event — different addresses, different damage, same storm' (3797), 'fell between the established coverage areas the way the pool and roses claim had fallen' (3901). The narrator's closing reflection (3913-3921) restates the chapter's insight ('Be unintentional on purpose. Try to not try. The problem was clear and the solution was its own impossibility') — this is thesis language that the bar conversation already delivered through dialogue. The 'the way' comparison appears ~9 times. KEEP the insurance metaphor at line 3797 ('same weather event — different addresses, different damage, same storm') — it is earned and emotionally precise. REPLACE elsewhere.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3689-3921. The closing section (3913-3921) should be cut by at least half. The explicit restatement 'Be unintentional on purpose. Try to not try. The problem was clear and the solution was its own impossibility' restates what Simone already said ('You need to not need') — cut the narrator's paraphrase and let Simone's line stand. The Simone observation section (3743-3800) uses 'the way she [verb]' at least 4 times in describing how Doug watches Simone — vary at least 2 of these into direct sensory detail (what he actually sees rather than how it compares to other watching). The extended insurance metaphor at line 3901 ('fell between the established coverage areas') extends the comparison past its landing — the sidewalk moment works through physical specificity, not through policy analysis. Cut or shorten the metaphor at 3901. Aim for no more than 4 'the way' comparisons in the full chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 3689-3921. The closing reflection (3913-3921) must be at least 4 lines shorter. The sentences 'Be unintentional on purpose. Try to not try.' must be cut — Simone's 'You need to not need' is the chapter's statement of this idea and must not be paraphrased by the narrator afterward. Count 'the way [noun] [verb]' comparisons: pass if 5 or fewer. The weather-event metaphor at 3797 must be preserved.

findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3932, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3936, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3953, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3966, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3978, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4016, snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4090
findings.problem: Prose formula saturation. Chapter 16 (the Lincoln Theatre rehearsal and dinner) concentrates the appositive-explanation pattern in the theater description and the dinner scene. The theater section uses 'the kind of' and 'which was' constructions to gloss nearly every physical detail: 'the smell of renovation that hadn't quite finished' (3930), 'work lights were on' followed by appositive about what work lights look like (3934), 'fine in the way that falling is fine for the first several floors, before the ground arrives' (3953). The 'photocopy of a photocopy' metaphor at line 3966 is strong but is immediately followed by another 'which was' explanation clause. The dinner scene (3974-4015) uses the negation-correction pair at least 4 times. KEEP the 'photocopy of a photocopy' at line 3966 and the 'fine in the way that falling is fine' at line 3953. REPLACE elsewhere.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3924-4122. The theater-description section (3924-3945) should be trimmed — the reader doesn't need every technical detail of the setup glossed with a qualifying clause. Let some details (the gaffer tape, the cameras, the sound board) land as facts without 'which was' or 'the kind of' explanations. The dinner scene's crayon-drawing detail (Layla's circles, lines 3978 and 4014-4016) is good physical business and should be preserved, but the narrator's commentary on what the circles 'suggest' (3978: 'suggested a mind that was organizing itself around an anxiety') can be cut — the circles are vivid enough without psychological narration. Aim for no more than 5 'which was' appositives and 3 negation-correction pairs in the full chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 3924-4122. Count 'which was' appositives: pass if 7 or fewer. The theater description (3924-3945) must contain at least 3 physical details that land without any qualifying or explanatory clause. The 'photocopy of a photocopy' at line 3966 must be preserved. Layla's crayon circles must be preserved as physical action but the narrator's psychological interpretation of them at line 3978 must be cut.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Kelsey's calibration-only line 'Do not go on the internet' appears verbatim at line 55 in Chapter 1. The style bible for kelsey_bridge_coyle lists this under example_lines with the header 'CALIBRATION ONLY — DO NOT COPY, PARAPHRASE, OR ECHO IN ANY DRAFT.'
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:55. Replace the verbatim line with a Kelsey-voice equivalent that conveys the same directive without echoing the calibration text. Options: a warning about looking at his phone, a specific reference to what she's seen online, or a terse instruction that carries her defensive humor. The replacement must use high-contraction register and sound like Kelsey under pressure — direct, clipped, not therapeutic.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 55 and surrounding context. The verbatim phrase 'Do not go on the internet' must not appear. The replacement must convey the same instruction (don't look at online reactions) in Kelsey's voice: high contraction, direct, with her characteristic mix of protectiveness and exasperation. The replacement must not introduce any other calibration-line echoes.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Charlie Falk's calibration-only line 'That's the POINT, Doug. That's the whole thing.' appears verbatim at line 1232. The style bible for charlie_falk lists this exact phrase under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1232. Replace with Charlie-voice enthusiasm that makes the same argument — that Doug's ordinariness IS the content — without echoing the calibration phrasing. Charlie's syntax is exclamatory, building, with dashes and restarts. The replacement should sound like Charlie mid-pitch: layering reframings, using 'like' as framing device, capitalizing verbally.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 1232 and surrounding context. The phrases 'That's the POINT' and 'That's the whole thing' must not appear verbatim. Charlie's replacement dialogue must still argue that Doug's ordinariness is the show, using his characteristic enthusiastic, building syntax with restarts and reframings.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Simone's calibration-only line 'Tell me something funny' appears verbatim at line 1367. The style bible for simone_barr lists this under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1367. Replace with a Simone-voice prompt that opens the same scene without echoing the calibration text. Simone's register is dry, direct, economical. Options: she could ask Doug to describe something specific, or give a diagnostic instruction ('Start with yesterday'), or simply wait and let the silence do the prompting — which fits her character's radar for inauthenticity.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 1367 and surrounding context. The phrase 'Tell me something funny' must not appear. The replacement must serve the same scene function (opening the first real working session) in Simone's voice: dry, direct, professional, with no extra words.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Brendan's calibration-only line 'I couldn't have done what you did. I want you to know that.' appears verbatim or near-verbatim at line 1837. The style bible for brendan_coyle lists 'I couldn't have done what you did. I want you to know that.' under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1837. Replace with a Brendan-voice expression of the same admiration that doesn't echo the calibration text. Brendan is warm, articulate, genuinely comfortable — he can say difficult things wrapped in enough warmth that they arrive gently. The replacement should capture his awareness that he occupies the 'winner' position in the triangle and that this line is his attempt to honor what Doug did. Options: Brendan could describe his own reaction to the toast specifically, or acknowledge the difficulty of what Doug said without the generic 'I couldn't have done' frame.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 1837 and surrounding context. The phrase 'I couldn't have done what you did' must not appear. The replacement must convey Brendan's genuine admiration for the toast in his voice: warm, articulate, slightly careful, demonstrating that he listened to what was said rather than delivering a generic compliment.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Simone's calibration-only line 'The material doesn't work. You know that.' is echoed at line 2112 where Doug says 'The material doesn't work.' The core phrase is verbatim from the style bible's simone_barr example_lines.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2112. Replace Doug's statement with a formulation that conveys the same realization without echoing Simone's calibration line. Doug would express this in insurance language — e.g., the material is a denied claim, or the documentation is right but the coverage isn't there. The replacement should feel like Doug processing the failure through his own vocabulary, not borrowing Simone's diagnostic phrasing.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 2112 and surrounding context. The exact phrase 'The material doesn't work' must not appear. Doug's replacement must convey the same realization (that the scripted set failed) using his characteristic insurance-adjuster framing or specific factual observation rather than Simone's diagnostic language.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Simone's calibration-only line 'You're boring when you try to be interesting' appears at line 2664 as 'You're boring when you try to be interesting. That's the whole diagnosis.' The style bible lists 'You're boring when you try to be interesting' under simone_barr example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2664. Replace with a Simone-voice diagnosis that makes the same point without echoing the calibration text. Simone's register here is diagnostic and blunt. Options: she could describe the difference she hears between Doug talking and Doug narrating, or she could point to the specific moment the energy died, or she could use a comedy-writer diagnostic term ('you went dead,' 'that's the performed version'). The replacement should feel like a professional observation, not a quotable line.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 2664 and surrounding context. The phrase 'You're boring when you try to be interesting' must not appear. The replacement must deliver the same diagnostic insight (that Doug's narrating/performing self kills the authenticity) in Simone's dry, direct voice without becoming a quotable aphorism.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Two of Doug's calibration-only lines appear verbatim in Chapter 12: 'I drive a Civic' at line 2824, and 'I bought better cereal' at line 3044. The style bible for doug_bridge lists both under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2824 and snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3044. For line 2824: replace Doug's deflection to the journalist's 'What drives you?' with a different Doug-specific literal misunderstanding — he could answer with a specific fact about his commute, his alarm clock, or his morning routine. The humor should come from the same source (treating an abstract question as a concrete one) without using the calibration phrasing. For line 3044: replace 'I bought better cereal' with a different specific, small change that carries the same weight — a different brand he switched to, a slightly upgraded item, something precise and Doug-specific that shows the change is real but tiny.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 2824 and 3044. The phrases 'I drive a Civic' and 'I bought better cereal' must not appear verbatim. Each replacement must serve the same function (Doug answering abstractly with concrete specifics) in Doug's voice: literal, precise, factual, slightly too detailed for the context.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Simone's calibration-only line 'The material doesn't work' is echoed verbatim at line 3656, spoken by Doug during the post-Detroit phone call. This is the second occurrence of this calibration phrase in the manuscript (also at line 2112 in Chapter 9).
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3656. Replace with Doug's own formulation of the same realization. By Chapter 14, Doug has failed three times — he should have his own vocabulary for this failure by now. Options: he could describe the material as a denied claim, a cosmetic fix over structural damage, or 'the set list is a filing system and nobody wants to watch someone file.' The replacement should show that Doug has internalized the diagnosis through his own language, not through Simone's.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 3656 and surrounding context. The phrase 'The material doesn't work' must not appear. Doug's replacement must name the same failure using insurance-adjuster or Doug-specific vocabulary that shows he has processed the three performances into his own diagnosis.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Simone's calibration-only line 'I'm funnier on paper. You're funnier in person. I don't know which is worse.' appears nearly verbatim at line 3795. The style bible for simone_barr lists this exact phrasing under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3795. Replace with a Simone-voice observation that makes the same comparison (her written craft vs. his accidental presence) without the three-sentence parallel structure of the calibration line. Simone is dry, economical, professional — the replacement should sound like something she'd say after two bourbons when the professional frame has relaxed. Options: she could describe what she envies about Doug's accidental comedy, or frame the comparison through the performers she's written for, or make it about the material ('Everything I write for you dies. Everything you say without me lives.').
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 3795 and surrounding context. The three-sentence parallel structure ('I'm funnier on paper. You're funnier in person. I don't know which is worse.') must not appear. The replacement must convey the same comparison in Simone's voice: dry, tired, genuine, with the slight vulnerability that surfaces late in the novel.

findings.problem: Style-bible example line leakage. Roy's calibration-only line 'That's the worst kind of bad' is echoed at line 4245. The style bible for roy_utley lists 'That's the worst kind of bad' under example_lines with the 'CALIBRATION ONLY' header.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4245. Replace with a Roy-voice assessment that carries the same weight in fewer or different words. Roy speaks in short declaratives, subject-verb-object, with no rhetorical questions or elaboration. The replacement should sound like Roy placing a fact in its correct location — perhaps a one-word judgment, a comparison to something at work, or simply silence followed by a subject change.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read line 4245 and surrounding context. The phrase 'the worst kind of bad' must not appear. Roy's replacement must sound like Roy: flat, unhurried, economical, delivering a verdict that requires no elaboration.

findings.problem: Timeline continuity error. Chapter 13 places itself in 'late April' at multiple points: 'Saturday in late April' (narration near 3259), 'Saturday in late April' (3297 area), and 'late April finally asserting itself against the long memory of winter' (3403). However, the immediately preceding Chapter 12 explicitly dates itself as 'a Tuesday morning in May' (line 2814). Since the chapters are sequential, Chapter 13 must take place AFTER Chapter 12's May date. The continuity sheet places the Kelsey visit at approximately week 16-17, which corresponds to late May or early June — not late April. The April references are a timeline contradiction that breaks the seasonal track.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3259, 3297, 3403. Replace all 'April' references in Chapter 13 with 'late May' or 'early June,' consistent with the chapter's sequential position after the May-dated Chapter 12 and the continuity sheet's week 16-17 timeline. At line 3403, update the seasonal description: instead of 'late April finally asserting itself against the long memory of winter,' adjust to reflect late May/early June weather in Columbus (warm, trees fully leafed, the sustained warmth that arrived in Chapter 14). The Thai restaurant details (the faded awning, the ceramic cat) are timeless and need no seasonal adjustment. Also check any other date-dependent details in the chapter for consistency.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read lines 3127-3420. No mention of 'April' should appear. All seasonal/temporal references must place the chapter in late May or later. The seasonal description at line 3403 must reflect late May/early June Columbus weather (warm, not transitional). The chapter must read as chronologically following the May-dated Chapter 12.

findings.problem: Dramatic recurrence. The three-city tour restages the same insight three times: in Cleveland (scripted material fails, the material-as-costume realization), in Pittsburgh (material fails, 90 seconds of the pool story work accidentally, then the return to material kills it), and in Detroit (material fails, heckler disrupts, 8 minutes of unscripted talk work, then the return to material kills it). The verdict is identical each time: spontaneity works, planning kills it. Cleveland and Pittsburgh deliver the same insight Detroit does, but with less dramatic pressure and no new information. The chapter also repeats a phone-call-after-performance template: each city ends with Doug calling Simone, the calls following the same ladder ('How was it?' / 'Bad.' / 'Bad how?' / specific diagnosis / Simone's brief comment). Cleveland contributes no unique dramatic content that Pittsburgh and Detroit don't deliver better. Pittsburgh's 90-second pool-story moment is valuable but could be folded into the Detroit section.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3422-3688. Option A (preferred): Cut the Cleveland section entirely (3422-3497, ~75 lines) and open the chapter with Pittsburgh. The Pittsburgh pool-story breakthrough and the Detroit heckler-to-unscripted sequence together deliver the full arc (failure → accidental breakthrough → failure when returning to script). This gives the chapter two distinct beats instead of three identical ones. Option B: Radically compress Cleveland into 10-15 lines of summary (a sentence on the venue, a sentence on the performance, a sentence on the result) and preserve Pittsburgh and Detroit as full scenes. For the phone calls: vary the post-show processing — not every city needs the same Simone call. Detroit's call is the strongest; Pittsburgh could end with Doug's solitary reflection instead.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read the full revised Chapter 14. The chapter must contain no more than two fully rendered performance scenes. If Cleveland is preserved, it must be 15 lines or fewer. The post-show processing must use at least two different formats across the remaining cities (not every one can end with a Simone phone call following the 'How was it? / Bad. / Bad how?' template). The total chapter length should be at least 50 lines shorter than current. The Detroit unscripted 8-minute segment must be preserved in full.

findings.problem: Chapter-level sprawl. Chapter 12 (the Alex Chen profile day) is the novel's longest chapter and restates the same insight — that the public Doug is an invention and the real Doug is unremarkable — across four distinct scenes (the apartment interview, the office, the lunch with Roy, the Simone session) and then again in Doug's post-profile reaction (3062-3124). The office scene (2884-2924) and the Kroger grocery scene (3010-3058) add texture but no new dramatic turns: Diane calling Doug 'iconic,' a college kid asking for a photo, and Beverly the cashier are all variations on 'strangers see the internet Doug, not the real Doug,' which the apartment interview already established. The post-profile reflection (3062-3124) then states this insight explicitly for a fourth time. The chapter's dramatic work — showing the gap between the article's Doug and the real Doug — is complete by the end of the Simone session. Everything after that is restatement.
findings.rewrite_direction: Target span: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2812-3126. Cut or radically compress one of: the office scene (2884-2924) or the Kroger scene (3010-3058). The Kroger scene's college-kid photo is charming but adds no dramatic turn the office scene doesn't already deliver. If the Kroger scene is kept, the 'I bought better cereal' exchange (which must be replaced per F14 anyway) should carry the scene's only new beat. The post-profile reflection (3062-3124) must be trimmed by at least a third — Doug's paragraph-by-paragraph correction of the article (3066-3078) makes the same point repeatedly ('the article said X, the reality was Y'). Pick the 2 strongest corrections, cut the rest. The chapter should lose at least 40 lines overall.
findings.acceptance_test: Re-read the revised Chapter 12. Total chapter length must be at least 40 lines shorter than current. Either the office scene or the Kroger scene must be significantly compressed (20+ lines shorter) or removed. The post-profile reflection (3062-3124) must contain no more than 2 article-vs-reality corrections (down from 5+). The chapter must still include: the apartment interview, the lunch with Roy (which is strong), and the Simone session. The Kroger 'cereal' exchange must use the revised (non-calibration) phrasing per F14.
